examples.title: Programmed I/O (Polling) Example
examples.description: Demonstrates how programmed I/O works by continuously checking device status.
examples.code:
PROCEDURE ReadFromDevice(device)
BEGIN
  REPEAT
    status ← READ_STATUS_REGISTER(device)
  UNTIL (status READY_BIT = TRUE)
  
  data ← READ_DATA_REGISTER(device)
  RETURN data
END
examples.explanation: The CPU continuously reads the device status register in a loop until the device is ready. This wastes CPU cycles but is simple to implement.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n depends on device response time

examples.title: Interrupt-Driven I/O Handler
examples.description: Demonstrates interrupt service routine for handling device input.
examples.code:
// Main program continues execution
MAIN_PROGRAM
BEGIN
  ENABLE_INTERRUPTS()
  PRINT("Program continues...")
  // CPU can do other work here
END

// Interrupt Service Routine
INTERRUPT_SERVICE_ROUTINE(device_id)
BEGIN
  SAVE_CPU_STATE()
  
  status ← READ_STATUS_REGISTER(device_id)
  IF status = DATA_READY THEN
    data ← READ_DATA_REGISTER(device_id)
    PROCESS_DATA(data)
  ENDIF
  
  ACKNOWLEDGE_INTERRUPT(device_id)
  RESTORE_CPU_STATE()
END
examples.explanation: Instead of polling, the CPU continues normal work. When the device is ready, it generates an interrupt, and the ISR handles the data transfer efficiently.

examples.title: DMA Transfer Operation
examples.description: Demonstrates Direct Memory Access for efficient data transfer.
examples.code:
PROCEDURE InitiateDMAtransfer(device, memory_address, byte_count)
BEGIN
  // Setup DMA controller
  WRITE_DMAC_REGISTER(SOURCE_ADDR, device_address)
  WRITE_DMAC_REGISTER(DEST_ADDR, memory_address)
  WRITE_DMAC_REGISTER(COUNT, byte_count)
  WRITE_DMAC_REGISTER(CONTROL, READ_FROM_DEVICE | START_TRANSFER)
  
  // CPU is now free to do other work
  // DMA controller handles the transfer
  // CPU will be interrupted when transfer completes
END

// DMA completion interrupt handler
DMA_COMPLETE_INTERRUPT
BEGIN
  WRITE_BUFFER_COMPLETE_FLAG(TRUE)
  NOTIFY_OPERATING_SYSTEM()
END
examples.explanation: DMA allows the device to transfer data directly to/from memory. The CPU sets up the transfer and then continues with other tasks, improving overall system performance.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for transfer, O(1) for CPU involvement

examples.title: Bootstrap Process in C
examples.description: Simplified demonstration of bootstrap program loading the OS.
examples.explanation: The bootstrap program initializes hardware, performs self-tests, loads the OS kernel from storage into memory, and transfers control to start the operating system.

examples.title: Cache Memory Simulation
examples.description: Demonstrates the concept of cache memory with hit/miss simulation.
examples.code:
class CacheSimulation:
    def __init__(self, cache_size=4, main_memory_size=16):
        self.cache = {}  # Simulated cache (LRU)
        self.cache_size = cache_size
        self.main_memory = {i: f"Data_{i}" for i in range(main_memory_size)}
        self.hits = 0
        self.misses = 0
    
    def access_memory(self, address):
        # Check cache first (fast access)
        if address in self.cache:
            self.hits += 1
            return f"CACHE HIT: {self.cache[address]}"
        
        # Cache miss - load from main memory
        self.misses += 1
        data = self.main_memory[address]
        
        # Add to cache (evict oldest if full)
        if len(self.cache) >= self.cache_size:
            # Simple LRU eviction
            oldest_key = next(iter(self.cache))
            del self.cache[oldest_key]
        
        self.cache[address] = data
        return f"CACHE MISS: Loaded {data}"
    
    def get_hit_rate(self):
        total = self.hits + self.misses
        return (self.hits / total * 100) if total > 0 else 0

# Demonstration
cache_sim = CacheSimulation()
addresses = [0, 1, 2, 0, 1, 3, 0, 3]

print("Memory Access Sequence:")
for addr in addresses:
    result = cache_sim.access_memory(addr)
    print(f"Address {addr}: {result}")

print(f"\nCache Hit Rate: {cache_sim.get_hit_rate():.1f}%")
examples.explanation: This simulation demonstrates temporal and spatial locality. Frequently accessed data stays in cache, reducing access time. The hit rate shows cache effectiveness.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for cache hit, O(n) for cache miss (n = cache size for eviction)
